title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a sailing instructor called Laura talking about an inclusive sailing club programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a sailing instructor called Laura talking about an inclusive sailing club programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A sailing instructor called Laura talks about an inclusive club programme.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Laura, an instructor at Clearwater Inclusive Sailing Club. Our aim is to give people with different levels of physical ability the chance to learn sailing in a safe and friendly environment. We are not based on the coast. Our boats are used on a sheltered lake that was once a [9]stone quarry[/9]. When work there ended, water filled the area naturally, and paths, changing facilities and safe launching points were later built around it. The lake is calm enough for beginners but large enough to make lessons interesting.||The club welcomes young people and adults. No sailing experience is required, and participants do not have to be strong swimmers because safety equipment and trained support are always provided. On the first morning, nobody sails immediately. The most important first activity is a [10]safety briefing[/10], where instructors explain life jackets, weather rules and how to ask for help. The club lends all special equipment. Learners should bring comfortable clothing and [11]waterproof trousers[/11], since spray can make people cold even when the weather seems warm on shore. Changing rooms are fully accessible.||We use several boats designed to be stable and easy to control. Wheelchair users can reach the water by following a wide ramp down to our floating [12]pontoon[/12], which rises and falls gently with the lake level. Participants may come alone, but beginners never sail without support. Each new sailor is matched with a trained [13]sailing partner[/13], who helps with equipment and encourages the learner to take as active a role as possible. Instructors also remain close by in small support boats throughout sessions.||Conditions on the lake can change quickly. Although staff check detailed forecasts, learners like knowing why decisions are made. Each morning we update a large [14]weather board[/14] outside the clubhouse, showing wind strength, temperature and whether all planned activities can take place. On days when sailing cannot go ahead, we offer practical indoor sessions. Last year people most enjoyed learning basic knots; this summer our optional activity will be a [15]photography competition[/15], with images taken from safe locations around the lake and judged at the final celebration.||Participants who attend regularly may progress beyond beginner sessions. Those completing the full summer programme receive an [16]achievement certificate[/16], and some return as volunteers the following year. We also run a training course for adults who want to help on the water. Successful learners can qualify as an [17]assistant instructor[/17], working alongside senior staff. Our club relies on fundraising to maintain safe equipment. This year, money from our open weekend will be used to purchase a new [18]rescue boat[/18], which can reach sailors quickly in any part of the lake. Programme dates and application details are in the leaflet you received, and I will be available for questions after the talk.

dialogue.speaker: laura
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Laura, an instructor at Clearwater Inclusive Sailing Club. Our aim is to give people with different levels of physical ability the chance to learn sailing in a safe and friendly environment. We are not based on the coast. Our boats are used on a sheltered lake that was once a stone quarry. When work there ended, water filled the area naturally, and paths, changing facilities and safe launching points were later built around it. The lake is calm enough for beginners but large enough to make lessons interesting.||The club welcomes young people and adults. No sailing experience is required, and participants do not have to be strong swimmers because safety equipment and trained support are always provided. On the first morning, nobody sails immediately. The most important first activity is a safety briefing, where instructors explain life jackets, weather rules and how to ask for help. The club lends all special equipment. Learners should bring comfortable clothing and waterproof trousers, since spray can make people cold even when the weather seems warm on shore. Changing rooms are fully accessible.||We use several boats designed to be stable and easy to control. Wheelchair users can reach the water by following a wide ramp down to our floating pontoon, which rises and falls gently with the lake level. Participants may come alone, but beginners never sail without support. Each new sailor is matched with a trained sailing partner, who helps with equipment and encourages the learner to take as active a role as possible. Instructors also remain close by in small support boats throughout sessions.||Conditions on the lake can change quickly. Although staff check detailed forecasts, learners like knowing why decisions are made. Each morning we update a large weather board outside the clubhouse, showing wind strength, temperature and whether all planned activities can take place. On days when sailing cannot go ahead, we offer practical indoor sessions. Last year people most enjoyed learning basic knots; this summer our optional activity will be a photography competition, with images taken from safe locations around the lake and judged at the final celebration.||Participants who attend regularly may progress beyond beginner sessions. Those completing the full summer programme receive an achievement certificate, and some return as volunteers the following year. We also run a training course for adults who want to help on the water. Successful learners can qualify as an assistant instructor, working alongside senior staff. Our club relies on fundraising to maintain safe equipment. This year, money from our open weekend will be used to purchase a new rescue boat, which can reach sailors quickly in any part of the lake. Programme dates and application details are in the leaflet you received, and I will be available for questions after the talk.
